presentations.title: Delirium / Acute Confusion / Encephalopathy
presentations.subtitle: Delirium and its encephalopathic mimics are acute medical emergencies needing same-hour vitals, glucose and precipitant work-up.

safetySnapshot.summary: Inattention plus altered awareness/arousal are the key separators. Usually precipitated by acute illness, surgery, injury, retention, constipation, pain, or medicines.

comparison.why-it-fits: Acute change, fluctuating course, inattention
comparison.must-not-miss: Sepsis, hypoxia, hypoglycaemia, stroke, intracranial bleed, alcohol/benzo withdrawal, urinary retention, faecal impaction.
comparison.bedside-question: Inattention plus altered awareness/arousal are the key separators. Usually precipitated by acute illness, surgery, injury, retention, constipation, pain, or medicines.
comparison.immediate-action: Do vitals, BGL, sats, attention testing, collateral, and medication/substance review early Use 4AT in most adult settings, and ICU tools where appropriate
comparison.investigations: Blood glucose, Vitals and oxygen saturation
comparison.mimics-overlap: Primary psychosis, dementia, severe depression, catatonia

presentations.title: Psychosis / First-Episode Medical Psychosis
presentations.subtitle: First-episode psychosis needs same-day assessment to exclude medical/substance causes; graded urgent unless medical instability or delirium escalates it to emergent.

safetySnapshot.summary: Inattention and fluctuation dominate. Psychotic content is often fragmented or changeable.

comparison.why-it-fits: New psychosis with fluctuating attention, visual hallucinations, confusion
comparison.must-not-miss: Sepsis, hypoxia, stroke, encephalitis, medication toxicity.
comparison.bedside-question: Inattention and fluctuation dominate. Psychotic content is often fragmented or changeable.
comparison.immediate-action: In older or medically unwell patients, assume delirium until proved otherwise Cannabis and stimulants can closely mimic primary psychosis
comparison.investigations: Neuroimaging (CT/MRI), EEG, Lumbar puncture / CSF studies

comparison.why-it-fits: Abrupt onset after cannabis, methamphetamine, cocaine, hallucinogens, steroids, or withdrawal
comparison.must-not-miss: Hyperthermia, chest pain, violent agitation, persistent psychosis.

presentations.title: Catatonia / Psychomotor Shutdown / Malignant Syndromes
presentations.subtitle: Malignant catatonia, NMS and serotonin toxicity are life-threatening; catatonia risks dehydration, VTE and autonomic instability.

safetySnapshot.summary: Characteristic motor syndrome rather than simple low mood, psychosis, or "behavioural refusal". Mood disorders are a very common substrate.

comparison.why-it-fits: Mutism, staring, posturing, negativism, waxy flexibility, stupor
comparison.must-not-miss: Refusal of intake, immobility complications, dehydration, autonomic change, hyperthermia, DVT/PE, rhabdomyolysis.
comparison.bedside-question: Characteristic motor syndrome rather than simple low mood, psychosis, or "behavioural refusal". Mood disorders are a very common substrate.
comparison.immediate-action: Use structured screen (Bush-Francis), examine motor signs, check vitals/hydration/CK, think benzodiazepine challenge early Catatonia is more often mood-linked than many clinicians expect
comparison.investigations: Vitals, oxygen saturation, Creatine kinase, Neuroimaging (CT/MRI), EEG, Lumbar puncture / CSF studies

comparison.why-it-fits: Psychosis plus marked motor syndrome
comparison.must-not-miss: Violence in excited catatonia, profound immobility, refusal of intake, autonomic instability.
comparison.bedside-question: Characteristic motor syndrome rather than simple low mood, psychosis, or "behavioural refusal". Mood disorders are a very common substrate.

presentations.title: Suicidal Ideation / Suicide Attempt / Self-Harm
presentations.subtitle: Active suicidality or a recent attempt requires urgent safety review and medical clearance.

safetySnapshot.summary: Sustained wish to die or escape, often with cognitive constriction and pessimism.

comparison.why-it-fits: Hopelessness, anhedonia, guilt, burdensomeness
comparison.must-not-miss: Planning, rehearsal, concealed intent, psychotic depression, high-lethality means.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained wish to die or escape, often with cognitive constriction and pessimism.
comparison.immediate-action: Quiet, organised suicidality is often higher risk than dramatic expression Mixed states are easy to miss and carry very high suicide risk

comparison.why-it-fits: Depression with agitation, irritability, racing thoughts
comparison.must-not-miss: Mixed features, recent antidepressant activation, psychosis, substance use.

presentations.title: Aggression / Violence / Homicidal Ideation
presentations.subtitle: Acute aggression or homicidal intent threatens immediate safety and may signal delirium, intoxication or mania.

safetySnapshot.summary: Aggression is often fear- or belief-driven rather than predatory.

comparison.why-it-fits: Paranoia, internal preoccupation, threat misinterpretation
comparison.must-not-miss: Weapons, command voices, victim-specific delusions, severe disorganisation.
comparison.bedside-question: Aggression is often fear- or belief-driven rather than predatory.
comparison.immediate-action: Violence in psychosis is often defensive from perceived threat Mixed irritability is easy to underestimate

comparison.why-it-fits: Irritability, grandiosity, limit-setting intolerance, impulsivity

presentations.title: Mania / Hypomania / Mixed States
presentations.subtitle: Acute mania carries high risk from impaired judgement, exhaustion and behavioural dyscontrol; mixed states elevate suicide risk.

safetySnapshot.summary: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.

comparison.why-it-fits: Elevated/irritable mood, reduced need for sleep, overactivity, grandiosity, pressured speech
comparison.must-not-miss: Psychosis, dangerous disinhibition, financial ruin, violence, exhaustion.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

comparison.why-it-fits: Less severe, no psychosis, but still clearly episodic
comparison.must-not-miss: Escalation to mania, mixed features, impulsive harm.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

comparison.why-it-fits: Depression with agitation, irritability, racing thoughts, insomnia
comparison.must-not-miss: Very high suicide risk, abrupt escalation, psychosis.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

comparison.why-it-fits: Stimulants, cocaine, methamphetamine, steroids, cannabis
comparison.must-not-miss: Hyperthermia, chest pain, prolonged psychosis, violent agitation.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

comparison.why-it-fits: Activation after antidepressant initiation, especially in latent bipolarity
comparison.must-not-miss: Escalating agitation, suicidality, psychosis.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

comparison.why-it-fits: Rapid reactive mood shifts, anger, emptiness
comparison.must-not-miss: Self-harm, substance misuse, aggression.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

comparison.why-it-fits: First manic-like presentation in later life, neurological signs, frontal disinhibition
comparison.must-not-miss: Stroke, brain tumour, dementia, thyrotoxicosis, corticosteroid exposure.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained episodic activation plus decreased need for sleep are the key separators.
comparison.immediate-action: Sleep change is one of the highest-yield discriminators Hypomania is commonly found only on careful retrospective history

presentations.title: Substance Intoxication
presentations.subtitle: Significant intoxication can cause airway compromise, hyperthermia or trauma and needs immediate ABC and toxidrome assessment.

safetySnapshot.summary: Toxidrome clues: pupils, respiratory rate, nystagmus, tremor, diaphoresis, sympathetic overdrive.

comparison.why-it-fits: Slurred speech, ataxia, disinhibition, aggression, nystagmus
comparison.must-not-miss: Head injury, aspiration, hypoglycaemia, respiratory depression.
comparison.bedside-question: Toxidrome clues: pupils, respiratory rate, nystagmus, tremor, diaphoresis, sympathetic overdrive.
comparison.immediate-action: Do not assume alcohol explains everything, especially after trauma Cannabis can produce anxiety, psychosis, and depersonalisation

comparison.why-it-fits: Conjunctival injection, slowed time perception, anxiety, paranoia, derealisation
comparison.must-not-miss: Severe panic, paranoia, self-harm, psychosis.

presentations.title: Substance Withdrawal
presentations.subtitle: Alcohol and benzodiazepine withdrawal can progress to seizures and delirium tremens within hours.

safetySnapshot.summary: Timing after last drink plus autonomic overdrive and tremor are classic.

comparison.why-it-fits: Tremor, sweating, anxiety, agitation, insomnia, seizures after cessation
comparison.must-not-miss: Seizures, delirium tremens, severe autonomic instability, Wernicke risk.
comparison.bedside-question: Timing after last drink plus autonomic overdrive and tremor are classic.
comparison.immediate-action: CIWA-Ar if suitable, vitals, BGL, electrolytes, seizure and DTs risk, thiamine need Use medical-first, trauma-informed withdrawal pathway
comparison.investigations: Blood glucose, Vitals, oxygen saturation, Urea, electrolytes, creatinine

comparison.why-it-fits: Anxiety, tremor, perceptual disturbance, insomnia, agitation, seizures

presentations.title: Akathisia / EPSE / Tremor / Sedation
presentations.subtitle: Akathisia and EPSE cause marked distress and adherence breakdown and elevate suicide risk; acute dystonia can need faster intervention.

safetySnapshot.summary: Subjective inner restlessness is the key feature, often with observable motor restlessness.

comparison.why-it-fits: Inner restlessness, pacing, rocking, inability to sit still after antipsychotic or antidepressant exposure
comparison.must-not-miss: Suicidality, aggression, treatment refusal, severe distress.
comparison.bedside-question: Subjective inner restlessness is the key feature, often with observable motor restlessness.
comparison.immediate-action: One of the most commonly missed and most distressing psychiatric side effects Bilateral onset after medication strongly suggests drug-induced parkinsonism

comparison.why-it-fits: Bradykinesia, rigidity, masked facies, resting tremor, reduced arm swing after antipsychotics or dopamine blockers
comparison.must-not-miss: Falls, aspiration, severe functional decline.

presentations.title: Hallucinations
presentations.subtitle: New hallucinations need same-day assessment to separate delirium and substance causes from primary disorders.

safetySnapshot.summary: Usually chronic, internally generated but experienced as alien, embedded in broader psychotic syndrome.

comparison.bedside-question: Usually chronic, internally generated but experienced as alien, embedded in broader psychotic syndrome.
comparison.immediate-action: Do not just ask "any voices" — clarify form, content, controllability, conviction Prominent visual or tactile hallucinations widen the toxic/organic differential

presentations.title: Depression
presentations.subtitle: Depression

safetySnapshot.summary: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.
tags: Major depressive disorder, Bipolar depression, Persistent depressive disorder
CLINICAL HINGE: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: Low mood and/or anhedonia with biological, cognitive, functional change for 2+ weeks
comparison.must-not-miss: Suicide risk, psychosis, catatonia, refusal of intake, severe self-neglect.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: Recurrent depression, early onset, family history, atypical features, antidepressant activation
comparison.must-not-miss: Mixed features, high suicide risk, antidepressant-induced switching.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: Chronic low mood, low self-esteem, pessimism, reduced vitality over years
comparison.must-not-miss: Hidden chronic suicidality, double depression.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: Severe depression with nihilistic, somatic, guilt, punishment, poverty delusions
comparison.must-not-miss: High suicide risk, refusal of food/fluid, catatonia.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: Temporal link to intoxication, withdrawal, or medication change. Caused by: Alcohol, cannabis, stimulants, benzos, steroids, isotretinoin, interferon, beta blockers
comparison.must-not-miss: Withdrawal suicidality, mixed intoxication, severe functional decline.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: First-onset depression with atypical cognitive/neurological features or systemic context. Look for: Hypothyroidism, Parkinson disease, stroke, Cushing, B12 deficiency, sleep apnoea
comparison.must-not-miss: Late-life first depression, focal signs, endocrine features.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

comparison.why-it-fits: Loss-centred, wave-like, preserved self-worth vs full syndromal depression
comparison.must-not-miss: Persistent functional collapse, suicidality, severe self-blame, psychosis.
comparison.bedside-question: Sustained mood syndrome with guilt, hopelessness, reduced energy, impaired concentration, change in sleep/appetite.

redFlagFlows.title: 1. Suicide
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess intent, plan, means, temporality, protective factors
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: Current plan, access to means, hopelessness, command hallucinations to self-harm, recent attempt, severe agitation, mixed state, intoxication

redFlagFlows.title: 2. Delirium
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess fluctuation, attention, acute change, underlying medical trigger
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: Sepsis, hypoxia, seizure, stroke, withdrawal, medication toxicity, metabolic derangement

redFlagFlows.title: 3. Catatonia
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess mutism, posturing, negativism, stereotypy, echophenomena, autonomic instability
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: Malignant catatonia, NMS, serotonin toxicity, dehydration, DVT/PE, aspiration, rhabdomyolysis

redFlagFlows.title: 4. Psychosis
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess onset, substance/medication exposure, neurological signs, cognitive function, perceptual abnormalities
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: First-episode medical red flags (autoimmune, metabolic, infectious, structural), severe self-neglect, violence, catatonia, command hallucinations

redFlagFlows.title: 5. Aggression
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess target, plan, weapon access, intoxication, psychosis command, recent escalation
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: Immediate violence risk, weapon access, homicidal intent, severe intoxication, command to harm, child/elder safety risk

redFlagFlows.title: 6. Withdrawal
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess substance type, last use, autonomic signs, tremor, seizure risk, delirium risk
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: Severe autonomic instability, seizure, delirium tremens, benzodiazepine/GHB withdrawal severity, Wernicke risk (alcohol), medical decompensation

redFlagFlows.title: 7. Medication toxicity / QT
redFlagFlows.bedsideQuestions: Assess recent medication changes, anticholinergic burden, QT interval, neuroleptic malignant features, serotonin syndrome signs
redFlagFlows.keyRedFlags: QTc prolongation, NMS, serotonin syndrome, anticholinergic delirium, lithium toxicity, clozapine adverse effects, severe EPS, falls
